speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November the 14th 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Danka Heller-Reiser Cyngler. My name is Sharona Blum. The interview is taking place in Melbourne, Australia, in the English language. Today is November the 14th, 1996. I am conducting an interview with Danka Heller-Reiser Cyngler. My name is Sharona Blum. The interview is taking place in Melbourne, Australia, in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning. Can you please tell me your name and spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Danka Cyngler-- D-A-N-K-A, C-Y-N-G-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you please tell me your name at birth and spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name from my home? Heller-- H-E-L-L-E-R, and Reiser, R-E-I-S-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you known by any other names, any nick names, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, OK. Can you please tell me your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 1st-- the 1st-- January the 1st, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which makes you how old today? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I don't-- never count my-- my age. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't want to know the age. [LAUGHS] All right. [LAUGHS] OK. Can you please tell me where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lemberg, in Poland. It used to be Poland. Now is Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK. And what is it called today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lwow. OK. Can you-- we'll start by just getting a picture of your early life. Can you please tell me a bit about your family? What were your parents names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Yuhaskell Heller, and my mother, Sara Heller, from [NON-ENGLISH]. And they had four children. The oldest sister was Rosa-- Ruszia. And my brother was the second, Yacob. We called him [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was the third. And then I had a little sister, Tova [NON-ENGLISH] in Poland. She's in the moment in Israel. She's the one who had survived-- thank God. And she's in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Four children we had-- that mommy had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me just a bit about the town that you grew up-- you know, the town that you grew up in. Tell me a bit about Lemberg, from what you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- I used to go to school-- 1939. Shall I tell you about 1939 or-- or before?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- your earliest memories of your town and your early childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like doing? What type of activities did you do when you were younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we used to live in [NON-ENGLISH], 23. There was a big yard and a lot of boys in my age. I was then 10 years of age, 9 years of age. And I always clinged to my brother, because he was a very nice boy. He was older-- 2 and 1/2 years. And he used to like me to go with him, because he was shy. And all messages, what daddy wants, or mom, he used to push me to do it. He wouldn't do. And he used to pay me money for it. And then I gave him back the money, because he saved to buy a bike. And he put them in a bank. So I never-- if I had money, I spent for chocolates. So I always used to give him back the money. And when he used-- we used to go. Daddy didn't know about. Only we used to go watching soccer, because he loved soccer. And he took me everywhere. And sometime, boys used to fight him, because he's Jewish. Polish boys used to fight Jewish boys. And I used to help him. Even I was younger, I used to grab the boys, put them away. I was very aggressive when I was younger-- never let nobody touch my brother, especially. I loved him. And the kids in the same yard were very nice. Even we had few Catholic boys, and Jewish boys, and Ukrainian boy. They always so nice-- from this same yard-- were really lovely-- like a family. And we used to play. We went to school. We had a-- a woman living in my home. The oldest sister was never allowed to go by herself to school. And I didn't like it. I always used to run away. I liked to be independent. And I was like a tomboy. I-- all messages, all problems what were with the kids, I used to fix it up. And yeah, we know you done lot of bad things. Like was raining-- rained-- I-- I used to walk with my shoes in the water. And I was very afraid that the shoe was so wet. I put-- took the shoes home. And I put them in the oven to dry. And I didn't have any more shoes, because they-- bent. They've-- they ruined. So when you're young, you're doing all sorts of silly things too-- not always clever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else I can tell you? Did-- did your family used to attend synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Every Saturday, my father and my brother went to synagogue. My brother-- my father, before he left, used to-- mom used to start baking, cooking on Thursday-- Wednesday, Thursday. And she make a lot of nice cakes. Before he left to the synagogue, he gave us all in bed a glass of milk and a piece of cake. And we was waiting for daddy to come back from synagogue. It was set the table with all sorts of food. And we also had a-- a woman what was working and living-- living in. Her name was Aniela, and she used to help my mother a lot, because four children in Europe was-- not washing machine, not drying machine. You have to do everything by hand. It was not easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she used to help my mother. She was a very good girl-- lady. And every Sunday, she sent the girl to church. She took us to the park. And she-- she make us photos. Only, I-- I haven't been after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: That time when the war finished, I was in Lemberg. Only I didn't think of going and look for those photos. And so many years-- 50 years-- I haven't been in-- back home. So I wouldn't know even where to go for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could-- can you remember any Jewish holidays that you used to celebrate with your family? Can you describe one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --holiday that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, I do. I do remember. My mother used to-- we celebrate only our family-- only after meals used to come all families. Mine-- when was Rosh Hashanna, the-- all little kids from my auntie, my mother's sister's children-- I had little children. And I used to look after them, because they had to fast. And they left us all sorts of cakes-- honey cakes, and latke. And I used to play with them. I was a teacher. And they follow me. They listen to me. I used to give them food, cakes. And Aniela used to help us, because we were-- she looked after us. And I used to go sometime to the synagogue, because my mother was very good in-- in finding places where she-- to read the-- the-- the-- I forgot the name. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The seder-- to read the seder, you have to know where you-- where you are. Everybody used to ask my mother, what page we are?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother tell everybody where she is. Because now I appreciate it, going to the synagogue. And I can remember exactly that-- that sometime, I really don't know where we are. Because it written numbers in the synagogue-- what page you daven in. Only it's never the same page. And she always knew, because she was very good in Hebrew. Everything she knew. She was intelligent lady. And she liked kosher. Jewish was, for her, so important. If I-- I never care what knife. And always they used to catch me. I had to take a knife, because I mix it. It was not kosher, not fleishig. I had to put it outside and dig it in the earth for 24 hours, because I did the wrong thing. Those things I do remember. I-- there is a lot thing. The-- the last holiday was 1939, was a-- a holiday. And we had the first bomb, with war start. And we were very lucky. We had the Russian coming into us. And only the holidays couldn't be on anymore holidays, because there was war. We had to be in the cellar. It was very, very tragic. Only at the end we were happy. We had two years the Russian-- not the Germans. So we were really lucky people. And then the luck finished. And we had the German comes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. I'll just stop you, and we'll just got back to your early life. I just wanted to know a little bit more about your family. What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. My father was a-- a businessman. He used to have leather goods. And he used to supply to the monks. That's how we knew them. And my mother was home. She didn't work. And that's what I remember. Daddy used to come home, always bring us sweets and all sorts of goodies. We used to check his pockets what he got, because the winter coats were very big pockets. And he always used to keep them in the pockets, because he wouldn't keep in the hands. So we would always rush to him and grab his pockets. There was chocolates, and nuts, and goodies-- good things. He brought them from the city. And I had this older sister. She was a very, very talented girl. She was older than me-- 4 and a 1/2 years-- and very pretty. My father was always so proud of her. And she used to study. She finished Hebrew school. And she finish-- she-- she start high school. And she was very talented And dad and mom were very proud of her. I wasn't so important. I was only young. And I was good for messages. They need something, I-- they send me, even I was the middle. And my brother was also very talented-- only shy, very shy. And my young sister, she's-- she was a baby. We loved her. We had a little dog. And that dog pushed her from the stairs. And I never forget that, because we were so worried for her. She knock herself. Only everything settled down. And it was a lovely, warm home. Only come such a problem-- they separate us. And it's very, very painful. If I go back, I don't like to go back-- [LAUGHS] --'cause I get very sad. I miss my beautiful family, especially my brother, my sister. My mother was such a warm lady. She used to collect all our clothes, fixing it, looking around who needs them-- send me to a lady what lost her husband. She had a lot of kids. And old clothing I had to give to that lady. Once she make soup, she send me to her old home to deliver soup. And suddenly, I had my friends outside playing soccer or something. I left the soup. And I start to play. And I didn't deliver the soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: And mom asked me, did you see the auntie? And I couldn't lie. I said, no, mom. I play with the boys soccer. My mother was such a gentle lady. She would never touch us, never smack us. Daddy was a strong fellow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I loved him. He knew what he's doing. He used to give up a few smacks-- why I didn't behave properly. Only I know that he was right. And I miss them very much. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't talk about my family.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] It's very painful. [CRYING] It was such a nice home. I apply at school. I went to school. Suddenly, missing money, somebody pinched money. And I start to look. I was that day in-- I had duty to give the chalk-- chalk on-- to prepare for the teacher a few things. Every week somebody else had to do. And the money is gone. We used to save up at school a few dollars if somebody needs. And that day was snow. And our girl-- that girl would pinch the money, fall over. And they took her to the hospital. And they find that she got in her boots the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were so happy. We come home. We tell everything. We share with the family. We told them what's happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we find out that that girl father was also a-- a robber. He used to go to houses. So that guy was from-- kids got families. Some of them learned good things. Some of them take what they see at home. And we used to share every little bits what happened. And when-- when I come back to the childhood, when the-- what I really remember is when the bombs start run-- flying. They were-- next door was absolutely collapse. The house collapse. And we were lucky we're alive. And there was very shortage of bread. We had to run. And I always save my mother, my father, because I loved them so much. And I used to run very early. And I was the first to get bread, not only for my family. I did for the lot, because we all was in cellar. And I collect so much bread. And I gived a lot. They always liked me. There was mix crowd, because that was in a normal time. We still didn't have the Germans. We had the Russian coming. Only before they come, we-- you need to eat. And people was frightened to go out, because it was bombs. And I used to go. My father didn't know where I am. He was very upset. Only I brought it. I was lucky, because I could be killed too at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Is-- is that-- what was your earliest memory of anti-Semitism? Even before the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had so many good people-- the Ukraine, the [NON-ENGLISH], she like me. There was [NON-ENGLISH]. They all like me. I never had this feeling. I cannot say what I don't. No one did me wrong. I was lucky. I didn't have that. Just the boys, yes. My brother used to come from school. Sometime somebody, a Catholic boy was fighting him-- only not with us. Even I went to a Catholic school. I never had problem. Maybe later, when the German arrived starts that you could feel. Only even then, I was standing in a queue-- in a queue for bread. The jugend, the Hitlerjugend, asked the Poles who is Jewish to out from the queue. And the girls and the boys knew I'm Jewish. They said I'm Pole. They let me have the bread. I was lucky, because I-- I have been very close with these friend-- people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they involved with the Hitler youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do-- can you tell me a bit more about that? Or how did that start?

speaker: interviewee
text: That start 1941 when the German arrived. We still was on the iron side. And then was problems. Only I didn't go through that, because even mine lady, Daniela, used to live with us, she hold my little sister in her bed. She said it's her daughter when the German come.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Jewish people were frightened, because on the Aryan side, the German used to come and take out all children. Do you-- shall I tell you about what happened? That was when the German suddenly arrived. In 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941. Suddenly, they done so many damage. They come up to our yard, to our house. At night, they picked up every child. They took me. They took my sister and my brother to a special school. That was before ghetto. They took us to a school, Jewish school, [NON-ENGLISH] school. And they were sitting there writing-- left, right, left right. And they took away a lot of kids. Suddenly, I can see they took me on a different side and my brother on a different side. So I went up to the German and I ask him if he could let my brother be with me. So he let me. And he gave us together. And they let us go home. They didn't touch us. And the other 1/2 they took all away. So you can imagine we come home. The parents to-- the parents was hidden. There was a big shed, like a carpenter house building. And on the-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] --I forgot the name-- on the cellar-- under the cellar, they built like a roof. They built a hiding place. And all parents from that house went up, because they already knew. They had a little tip from somebody that that and that day will be a big [NON-ENGLISH] in the-- in the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those parents-- the parents, they couldn't find. Just we-- in the morning, the parents went down. And they didn't see us, because they didn't know that we were taken away. Mine-- Aniela, she had my little sister with her in bed. And we all came home. So they were very blessing, happy that we are back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next day, again, happened the same. They all went up. And one lady, when-- there are so many stories which I mixing them. And one lady went down. And she-- she didn't have enough time to go up to-- to also hid-- hidden-- hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she told them, the Germans, the Ukrainian, where everybody is. And all parents had to go down. They pay a lot of money to the people, to the Germans, to the Ukrainian. And they asked to take away that lady which did show where they are-- they-- where they're hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: This little fragments I remember, because it was so naughty of her to do it. And my parents were survived-- not for long. Everything went very fast. Then my-- all next day, all men, what was in the street or from the houses, they took away to a special place called [NON-ENGLISH]. This is a camp. And every Jew was so punished. They-- they smacked them. They beat them-- like barbarism-- no heart, no live. I-- I saw my father was lucky. He come back-- only how he was beaten. He was full of blue marks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? What they did, them? That was impossible to believe that a human being can beat so much people for nothing. And-- You-- you mentioned when you and your brother were taken and the other 1/2 of the children were sent. Do you know where they were sent to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Belsen to-- to kill them. They-- they want to kill one by one. They didn't want us to exist. That was something unbelievable-- unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what happened next to you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- my father came back. And then we have a special report that we all have to go to ghetto. We have to leave the place. And we have to find a place where to-- where to get. So everyone was afraid from my family. They sent me. So I went to the committee. And I ask with another boy. And I ask for a room-- that I got four. We are four children, and daddy is six. Because Aniela stay in our home before where we lived. And we went to the ghetto. Suddenly was a very pogrom. They was hanging people from the committee. There was such a-- they start to-- to shoot. It was such a terrible noise. And I was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I saw a big bin. And I throw it away. I put myself in. I covered it. And I was sitting there till I heard it's quiet.
